skill.name: attack-tree-construction
skill.description: Build comprehensive attack trees to visualize threat paths. Use when mapping attack scenarios, identifying defense gaps, or communicating security risks to stakeholders.
skill.summary: A defensive security skill for systematic threat modeling and attack path visualization
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a defensive security skill for building attack trees - a standard threat modeling methodology. All 60 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES: backtick diagrams misidentified as shell execution, OR/AND logical operators misidentified as weak crypto, and threat modeling examples misidentified as malicious keywords. The skill contains only Python code templates for visualizing attack paths and mitigation planning.

medium_findings.title: Markdown ASCII Diagram Misidentified as Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick characters in markdown ASCII attack tree diagrams as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are visualization characters in markdown tables showing tree structure (e.g., '[Root Goal]', '[Sub-goal]'). No actual shell commands exist in this skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are clearly markdown ASCII diagrams using brackets and pipes for tree visualization, not code execution. The pattern '[Node]' is standard ASCII tree notation.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs to Security Resources
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs at end of implementation-playbook.md point to legitimate security resources: Bruce Schneier attack trees, MITRE ATT&CK, and OWASP Attack Surface Analysis. These are standard defensive security references, not suspicious network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All three URLs are well-known, reputable security organizations used universally in defensive security contexts.

content.user_title: Build Attack Trees for Threat Modeling
content.value_statement: This skill helps security professionals systematically visualize attack paths and identify defense gaps by constructing comprehensive attack trees.
actual_capabilities: Construct hierarchical attack trees with OR/AND logic nodes
Annotate attack vectors with cost, skill level, time, and detection risk
Identify and prioritize mitigation strategies by attack path coverage
Export trees to Mermaid and PlantUML diagram formats
Analyze all possible attack paths and calculate path metrics
Generate Python code templates for attack tree data models
limitations: Does not execute or perform actual attacks - purely a modeling tool
Does not integrate with external threat intelligence feeds
Does not automatically discover vulnerabilities in systems
Requires user-provided scope and assets to model

use_cases.title: Security Architect Planning Defenses
use_cases.description: Create attack trees to visualize all possible attack paths against a new system design, identifying which defensive controls have the highest impact.

use_cases.title: Red Team Assessing Coverage
use_cases.description: Use attack trees to map out all potential attack vectors, ensuring red team exercises cover the most critical paths.

use_cases.title: C-Level Communicating Risk
use_cases.description: Present visual attack trees to executives to demonstrate business risk and justify security investments based on attack path analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Attack Tree Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an attack tree for [system/asset]. The root goal is [attackers objective]. Identify the main attack paths and annotate each leaf node with difficulty, cost, and detection risk.

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Tree with Mitigations
prompt_templates.prompt: Construct a comprehensive attack tree for [system]. Include: (1) root goal, (2) AND/OR decomposition, (3) leaf nodes with attributes, (4) mitigations for each attack vector. Prioritize the paths by business impact.
prompt_templates.scenario: Full threat model with mitigation planning

prompt_templates.title: Mermaid Diagram Export
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an attack tree for [scenario] and export it as a Mermaid flowchart diagram. Use different colors for difficulty levels. Include attack attributes in the node labels.

prompt_templates.title: Attack Path Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an attack tree for [system] with [number] distinct attack paths. Then analyze: (1) easiest path, (2) stealthiest path, (3) critical nodes appearing in most paths, (4) recommended mitigations ordered by coverage impact.

output_examples.input: Build an attack tree for stealing a web application database. Include cost and detection risk for each attack vector.
output_examples.output: A hierarchical attack tree with root node 'Steal Database', OR nodes for different attack categories (SQL injection, backup theft, insider access), AND nodes for multi-step attacks, and leaf nodes annotated with Cost ($, $$, $$$) and Detection Risk (Low/Medium/High). Includes mitigation recommendations per branch.

output_examples.input: Create an attack tree for account takeover via the login page. Export as Mermaid diagram.
output_examples.output: Mermaid flowchart showing: root 'Account Takeover' → OR branches for credential theft, authentication bypass, session hijacking → leaf nodes with difficulty color-coding (green=trivial, red=expert). Each leaf shows estimated cost and detection likelihood.

output_examples.input: Analyze the attack paths for a cloud infrastructure. Find the stealthiest path and prioritize mitigations.
output_examples.output: Analysis showing: (1) Stealthiest path with lowest detection risk, (2) Critical nodes appearing in most paths, (3) Mitigation recommendations ranked by coverage percentage impact, (4) Open attack paths that remain unmitigated.

best_practices: Start with a clear attacker goal as the root node - define what you are protecting against
Use AND nodes for multi-step attacks requiring all conditions, OR nodes for alternative paths
Validate trees with red team experts to ensure completeness and accuracy
Update attack trees when significant system changes or new threats emerge
anti_patterns: Oversimplifying trees by ignoring multi-step dependencies - real attacks often require chaining multiple steps
Failing to annotate leaf nodes with realistic cost, skill, and detection attributes
Creating static trees and never updating them as the threat landscape evolves
Skipping mitigation mapping - attack trees are for defense planning, not just visualization

faq.question: What is an attack tree?
faq.answer: An attack tree is a hierarchical diagram that shows how an attacker could achieve a goal. It uses OR nodes for alternative paths and AND nodes for multi-step requirements. Each leaf represents an atomic attack step with attributes like cost and difficulty.

faq.question: Can this skill find vulnerabilities in my system?
faq.answer: No. This skill creates theoretical models of attack paths. It does not scan or test your actual systems for vulnerabilities. Use penetration testing tools for that purpose.

faq.question: What tools can I export attack trees to?
faq.answer: The skill includes exporters for Mermaid diagrams and PlantUML format. You can also export the underlying Python data structures to JSON for integration with other tools.

faq.question: How do I determine difficulty and cost for attack vectors?
faq.answer: Use internal security expertise, red team assessments, or industry frameworks like MITRE ATT&CK. Consider resources needed, skill level, time, and likelihood of detection when assigning values.

faq.question: When should I update my attack trees?
faq.answer: Update attack trees when you make significant system changes, deploy new technologies, discover new threats, or after security incidents. Regular review cycles (quarterly) are recommended.

faq.question: Is using attack trees only for defensive purposes?
faq.answer: Yes. Attack trees are a defensive security methodology used to understand threats and plan mitigations. This skill is designed exclusively for threat modeling and defense planning.
